summary: Research verified terms and data depth for seeding "league hub" pages in three segments. (1) J-League: the official J.League Data Site (data.j-league.or.jp) and Digital Data Book are rich (J1/J2/J3 fixtures, standings, per-player appearance records, scorer lists) but their terms prohibit reproduction WITHOUT permission "regardless of profit or non-profit purpose" — copyright is claimed by J.League/J.League Data Center; Football LAB (DataStadium) explicitly allows only personal non-commercial use with attribution; FBref covers only J1+J2 (no J3), lost its Opta advanced-data feed in Jan 2026, and Sports Reference's policy expressly bans building sites/tools from scraped data; StatsBomb open data contains NO Japan competitions (verified competitions.json); the Hudl/StatsBomb free J1-2024 dataset is signup-gated with unstated (presumably evaluation) terms; the JFL site has only standings/results/top-scorer pages and was unreachable from a US network. Cleanest J-League seed = the already-licensed API-Football feed (J2/J3 fixtures+lineups let you derive appearances/starts; no minutes/goals detail) plus a DataStadium/J.League Data Center license if richer stats are wanted. (2) US college: stats.ncaa.org + NCAA LiveStats (Genius Sports, exclusive NCAA data partner through 2032) hold full box scores and player season stats (GP/GS, minutes, goals, assists, shots, SOG, saves, cards) for D1/D2/D3 men+women, updated near-real-time in season; NAIA/NJCAA equivalents on PrestoSports; but NCAA.com TOS and Sidearm TOS both ban commercial reproduction — US seeding rests on the strong Feist facts-not-copyrightable doctrine, executed carefully (no logos, no bulk TOS-bound scraping; license via Genius Sports for clean feeds). No collegefootballdata.com equivalent exists for soccer — only the unofficial henrygd/ncaa-api (MIT, scrapes ncaa.com, no legal cover). (3) Japanese university (JUFA): regional federation sites (jufa-kanto.jp via football-system.jp, jufa-kyusyu.jp etc.) publish results, official match records, scorer/assist rankings — no minutes, no API, no commercial provider; effectively zero structured player stats. SoccerTrack v2 (10 full-pitch 4K Japanese university matches, CC BY 4.0 — commercial use allowed) is a free asset to train/demo the video pipeline for exactly this segment. GTM: seedable richness ranks US college >> J2/J3 > JFL ≈ JP university; "video reports are the ONLY stats option" is literally true for JFL, JUFA, and NCAA D3/NAIA/NJCAA; legal cleanliness favors Japan-lower-division seeding via the existing API-Football license and permission-based partnerships with JFL/JUFA (small, single offices), while J-League official-site and Football LAB scraping is the highest-risk path and should not be done without a license.
jleague_sources: J-LEAGUE SOURCES AND TERMS. (1) data.j-league.or.jp (J.League Data Site): server-rendered official site covering J1/J2/J3 (plus Levain Cup, playoffs) with fixtures/results, standings, per-player appearance records (出場記録, /SFPR01/), scorer lists, hat-tricks, venue/weather splits, registration changes, suspensions — i.e., per-player per-match appearance data DOES exist publicly for J2/J3, richer than API-Football for those tiers. Terms: the J.League copyright page (jleague.jp/general/copyright.html) states copyright in documents/images/data belongs to J.League, J.League Inc., the J.League Data Center and affiliates, and reproduction is prohibited without permission "regardless of whether the purpose is for profit or non-profit"; the Digital Data Book (ddb.j-league.or.jp, covers J1/J2/J3 + 2026 Centennial Vision format, individual appearance/scoring/discipline records) carries the same notice (無断での複製・転載を禁止). Legal posture: Japanese copyright law doesn't protect bare facts, but Japan recognizes creative-database copyright (Art. 12-2) and tort liability for wholesale database copying (Tsubasa System line of cases), and the site terms form a contract — commercial scraping of the official data site is HIGH RISK; the sanctioned route is licensing via the J.League Data Center / DataStadium. (2) Football LAB (football-lab.jp, operated by DataStadium Inc.): advanced J1/J2/J3 team+player metrics (e.g., Chance Building Points). Terms (/pages/rules) verified: reproduction/public transmission/reposting prohibited without permission; an explicit carve-out allows individuals/bloggers to cite data ONLY if non-monetized with attribution; "commercial use and media publications require separate permission." Not usable for seeding without a DataStadium deal — but DataStadium is the obvious commercial licensing partner for J2/J3 stats. (3) FBref / Sports Reference: covers J1 (comp 25) and J2 (comp 49) only — no J3 section found (site:fbref.com search returns no J3 pages). In January 2026 Opta/Stats Perform terminated FBref's advanced-data feed (after Opta's FIFA WC2026 betting-data deal); FBref retains basic stats only. Sports Reference's data-use policy explicitly prohibits "creating websites or tools based on data scraped from their sites," prohibits AI training without permission, rate-limits to 10 req/min, and notes some upstream licenses preclude ANY redistribution. FBref is contractually and practically unusable for commercial seeding. (4) StatsBomb open data (github.com/statsbomb/open-data): competitions.json verified — NO Japan/J-League competitions. Separately, Hudl/StatsBomb released a free "J1 League 2024" dataset (event data + Hudl physical data) behind a signup form (hudl.com/gc/download/free-data-japans-2024-j1-league-en); license terms are not stated on the page — assume evaluation/research, not a commercial seeding right; it is J1 anyway, not the J2/J3 target. (5) JFL (jfl.or.jp): official site exposes fixtures/results, standings (順位), and top-scorer rankings (得点ランキング) via s.php pages; no structured player appearance/minutes data, no API; the site refused connections from a US IP during testing (likely geo-restricted) — operationally relevant. Third parties (FootyStats, Gekisaka, STADIO) carry standings/results only. With 16 clubs and one league office, the realistic path is permission, not scraping. (6) API-Football (already licensed, commercial use permitted under existing subscription): J1 full player stats; J2/J3 fixtures + lineups — lineups allow deriving appearances, starts, and substitute usage per player even without stat feeds; JFL fixtures only. This is the only commercially clean machine-readable seed for J2/J3 today.
us_college_sources: US COLLEGE SOURCES AND TERMS. (1) stats.ncaa.org / NCAA.com: official NCAA stats system covers D1, D2, D3 men's AND women's soccer with team schedules/results, game box scores, individual game-by-game and season stats. Per the official NCAA Soccer Statisticians' Manual (fetched and parsed): the official scorekeeper records lineups, substitutions (entry times), goals, assists (2pts/1pt scoring), shots, shots on goal, goalkeeper saves, goals against, yellow/red cards, fouls (committed and drawn), corners, offsides, PKs, attendance — and minutes played is an official per-player stat (GAA is defined as goals x 90 / actual minutes; the manual's rulings reference entering "0 under minutes played" for non-participants, confirming field-player minutes are recorded). So full apps/starts/minutes/goals/assists/shots/cards season lines are seedable for thousands of programs. Freshness: data flows from schools via NCAA LiveStats (Genius Sports), free to members, ~70,000 games/year, near-real-time during the fall season. Terms: NCAA.com TOS prohibits reproducing, creating derivative works from, or commercially exploiting "NCAA Content" which is defined to include statistics and scores; the NCAA's exclusive official-data distribution partner is Genius Sports (partnership extended through 2032; exclusivity framed around sportsbooks/AGL program, but Genius is the door for commercial data licensing). (2) Sidearm Sports (powers most school athletics sites + live stats): TOS verified — "The Services shall be used only in a noncommercial manner" and content may not be modified/published/reproduced; content "owned or controlled by SIDEARM Sports or the party credited." Commercial scraping of team sites is a contract violation. PrestoSports has the same role for smaller schools. (3) United Soccer Coaches: weekly Tuesday national + regional rankings for NCAA D1/D2/D3, NAIA, NCCAA, and NJCAA, men and women — rankings are facts, widely republished with attribution; low-risk seed content and a useful page-freshness signal. (4) APIs/datasets: there is NO collegefootballdata.com equivalent for soccer — confirmed gap. Closest: henrygd/ncaa-api (free, MIT, proxies/scrapes ncaa.com endpoints incl. the soccer stats/scoreboard paths; no legal disclaimers, inherits NCAA TOS risk); SportsDataverse has no college soccer package (softballR and nwslR exist, soccer doesn't); Kaggle only has one-off scrapes; TopDrawerSoccer aggregates college soccer stats commercially (evidence that the facts-based posture is operable in practice). (5) NAIA: naiastats.prestosports.com — PrestoStats is the NAIA's official platform and member schools are REQUIRED to submit individual and team stats for men's and women's soccer (leaders, scoreboard, season stats). NJCAA: njcaastats.prestosports.com — PrestoSports is the official stats/web provider; coverage thinner and patchier. LEGAL POSTURE: in the US, Feist means the underlying stats (facts) are not copyrightable, and hiQ narrows CFAA exposure for public pages; the residual risks are breach-of-TOS (contract) for automated bulk collection and trademark issues for school logos/marks. Realistic commercial postures, in order of cleanliness: (a) license a feed via Genius Sports; (b) seed compiled facts (names, GP, G, A, minutes) without logos, sourced/attributed, collected at low volume from public pages — defensible but not risk-free; (c) bulk scraping of stats.ncaa.org/Sidearm sites against TOS — avoid.
jp_university_sources: JAPANESE UNIVERSITY (JUFA) SOURCES. (1) Structure: national federation JUFA (jufa.jp) + regional federations run the leagues — JUFA Kanto (jufa-kanto.jp), Kansai, Kyushu (jufa-kyusyu.jp), etc. (2) What exists online: JUFA Kanto publishes fixtures/results and official match records (試合日程・結果・公式記録, hosted on the football-system.jp platform), standings, win-loss matrices (星取表), scoring rankings, assist rankings, and discipline/suspension lists for Div 1 and Div 2; official per-match records (公式記録) in Japanese university football conventionally include lineups, substitutions, and goal times (web pages/PDFs); Kyushu publishes scorer rankings per division; quality and structure vary significantly by region; everything is Japanese-only, no API, no English mirror. The jufa-kanto.jp footer does include a 利用規約 (terms) link and © JUFA Kanto notice. (3) Third party: soccer-db.net (hobbyist DB) compiles per-team player appearance records for university leagues — proof the data CAN be assembled from official records, but it is itself an unlicensed compilation, not a seed source. (4) No commercial data provider (Opta, StatsBomb, DataStadium, API-Football) covers JUFA at all — there are no minutes, no event data, no player season stat lines anywhere structured. (5) SoccerTrack v2 (arxiv 2508.01802, atomscott.github.io/SoccerTrack-v2): 10 full-length Japanese university-level matches, panoramic full-pitch 4K (BePro), ~900 minutes, per-frame game-state-reconstruction labels (2D pitch coordinates, jersey IDs, roles, teams) + 12-class ball-action labels; dataset CC BY 4.0, code MIT — COMMERCIAL USE ALLOWED with attribution; distributed via Hugging Face (atomscott/soccertrack-v2). This is directly usable to (a) train/validate the video pipeline on exactly this footage domain (non-broadcast, full-pitch, university level) and (b) build authentic demo reports for the segment without fabricating anything. The SoccerTrack Challenge 2025 (Nagoya University) ecosystem is also a warm intro channel to university clubs. (6) Realistic options: seed thin pages (team, division, standings, scorer rankings with attribution) and pursue a JUFA/regional-federation partnership — federations are small offices and the product gives their member clubs something no one else will build; university football matters commercially in Japan because J-League clubs recruit heavily from it (特別指定選手 / designated special player system), so longitudinal player tracking has scouting buyers downstream.
gtm_assessment: GTM ASSESSMENT — "seeded demo pages + claim your team". (a) SEEDABLE DATA RICHNESS: US college is far richest — full official player season lines (GP/GS, minutes, goals, assists, shots, SOG, saves, cards) for D1/D2/D3 men+women plus NAIA/NJCAA on PrestoSports, refreshed near-real-time in season; enough to seed credible player pages and even per-90 radar stats with zero video. J2/J3 is second — via the ALREADY-LICENSED API-Football feed you can seed full team pages, squads, fixtures, and per-player appearance/start counts derived from lineups (no goals/minutes detail without a DataStadium license; the official J-League data site has them but is contractually off-limits to scrape). JFL and JUFA are thinnest: standings, results, and scorer rankings only. (b) GAP THE VIDEO PRODUCT UNIQUELY FILLS (where video-derived reports are the ONLY stats option): JFL and Japanese university are the purest cases — NO commercial provider covers them and none ever will at their economics; for JFL clubs and JUFA programs, uploading match video is literally the only way any player stat beyond goals will ever exist. The same is true of NCAA D3/NAIA/NJCAA in a weaker form: basic box scores exist but zero event data, zero per-90/radar analytics, and thin Wyscout/InStat coverage — "your box score says 2 shots; our report shows every action" still lands. At J2/J3 the gap is narrower (clubs receive official DataStadium data through the league), so the wedge is affordability, self-serve analysis of academy/B-team/loan players, and longitudinal tracking across uploads — aligned with the existing Academy Watch tracking engine. NCAA D1 is weakest: Hudl/Wyscout incumbency and bigger budgets. (c) LEGAL CLEANLINESS RANKING OF SOURCES (cleanest to most dangerous): 1. API-Football (existing commercial license) — clean today for J1/J2/J3/JFL fixtures-level seeding. 2. SoccerTrack v2 (CC BY 4.0) — clean for pipeline training and authentic Japanese-university demo reports. 3. United Soccer Coaches rankings with attribution — facts, low risk. 4. Hand-compiled facts from US college box scores without logos (Feist facts doctrine) — defensible; avoid bulk TOS-bound scraping; Genius Sports is the licensing door if/when volume matters. 5. JFL and JUFA official sites — thin data, standard copyright notices, but tiny organizations where permission is realistically obtainable and partnership IS the GTM. 6. stats.ncaa.org / Sidearm / Presto bulk scraping — contract risk, do at low volume or not at all. 7. data.j-league.or.jp / Digital Data Book — explicit prohibition regardless of profit/non-profit + Japanese database-tort exposure; do not scrape; license via J.League Data Center/DataStadium if needed. 8. Football LAB and FBref — explicitly prohibited for commercial reuse (and FBref just lost its own upstream advanced data to a licensing dispute — a cautionary tale for building on scraped feeds). SEGMENT RANKING (composite): #1 Japan lower-division (J3+JFL): seeding is legally clean via the license you already hold, the claim-your-team motion maps to ~16 JFL + 20 J3 clubs reachable through two league offices, and at JFL the video report is the only stats option in existence; #2 Japanese university: weakest seed data but the strongest "only option" pitch, zero incumbents, a commercially-licensed training dataset (SoccerTrack v2) purpose-built for the footage domain, and a scouting-pipeline story (university-to-J-League) that fits Academy Watch's longitudinal tracking DNA — execute via federation partnership, not scraping; #3 US college: best seeded pages and the largest market (thousands of programs), but the video product's uniqueness is lowest in D1 — if entered, target D3/NAIA/NJCAA where "no analytics exist" still holds, seed facts-only pages without marks, and treat Genius Sports licensing as the scale path. Strategic note: the seeded-pages mechanic works best precisely where data is moderate-but-incomplete (US college, J2/J3 — credible page, visible gap); where data is near-zero (JFL, JUFA) the seeded page is mostly a claim-target shell, so lead with the demo video report (SoccerTrack-derived for Japan) rather than the stats hub.
